name: Mozarabic (Andalusi Romance)

ancestors.notes: Descent from the Vulgar/Late Latin of Hispania Baetica and the Levante; the most archaic surviving Ibero-Romance branch (Barbato & Minervini 2024).

Andalusi Arabic adstrate: the language of the dominant society under Islamic rule, source of the Arabic script the kharjas are written in, of loan lexicon (al-habib), and of the phonetic clues (apocope, non-diphthongization, retained voiceless stops) that the records preserve.
Pre-Roman Iberian substrate of the south and east.

stress.notes: Mozarabic kept Latin stress placement; oxytones on a final consonant (corachón, doler, tornarad), paroxytones elsewhere. Falling diphthongs /aj aw/ are attested (yannayr, sollayr); rising diphthongization of tonic Ĕ/Ŏ is largely ABSENT (bene, bono, mele — Barbato & Minervini 2024:158-160).

notes: Mozarabic / Andalusi Romance (8th-13th c. CE): the Ibero-Romance dialect bundle of al-Andalus, the L1 of much of the population in the early centuries before Arabization, and the oldest documented Ibero-Romance (Vicente 2020 §2.1). It has NO native orthography: it is written in Arabic and Hebrew consonantal scripts (the kharjas — 68 known, 42 in Arabic and 26 in Hebrew script), in botanical/medical glosses (Abulḫayr, Ibn Quzmān's zajal), in Toledo Mozarab charters, and in toponymy and Ibero-Romance loans. This spec therefore uses a ROMANIZED-Romance grapheme layer — the scholarly Latin transliteration convention of García Gómez, Galmés de Fuentes and Corriente for the kharjas — NOT the source Semitic scripts (an earlier stub carried an empty Arabic-script grapheme set and transcribed to nothing; this is the PR #574 design fix). RECONSTRUCTED features (Barbato & Minervini 2024; Galmés de Fuentes 1983): (1) conservative 5/7-vowel system, largely NO tonic diphthongization of Ĕ/Ŏ (bene, bono, not *biene/*bueno) — Arabic contact is argued to have arrested an incipient diphthongization; the metaphonic welyos 'eyes' is an exception. (2) Prevocalic F- RETAINED as [f] (filyo, farina — not Castilian h-). (3) Latin -LY-/-CL- → [ʎ] (filyo, welyos), not Castilian [x]. (4) ge,i and j → palatal [ʝ]/[ɟ] written ⟨y⟩ (yana < janua, yermanellas). (5) Intervocalic voiceless stops -t- -k- -p- largely CONSERVED unvoiced (matar, bokella, sabes) — the archaic Ibero-Romance trait shared only with High Aragon; final-vowel apocope (fač, kuand, mib). (6) Andalusi Arabic adstrate lexicon and the ⟨ḥ⟩=[ħ] of loanwords (habib 'beloved', al-habib). QUALITY: reconstructed — Andalusi Romance is known only from fragmentary, discontinuous, consonant-script records; vocalism and stress are editorial reconstructions, and dialect variation (diphthongizing Toledo/Zaragoza vs. non-diphthongizing West/Levante) is real. The gold is engine-pinned to this romanized layer; scholarly IPA and any spec/engine divergence are recorded as PR leads, not bent into the transcriptions.
